1.label: Question 1
1.scenario: Company A is a small health and care insurer specialising in accelerated and standalone Critical Illness (CI) insurance products. The Marketing Director of Company A has proposed to incorporate tiered benefits into these products.

i.question: Define tiered benefits in the context of CI insurance products.

Indicative Solution: Tiered benefits in Critical Illness (CI) insurance refer to a structure where different levels of payouts are offered depending on the severity of the condition diagnosed.

Instead of a single lump sum benefit being paid out for all qualifying conditions, a partial benefit may be paid for early-stage or less severe conditions, while a full benefit is reserved for more severe conditions or the later stages of the illness.
For example, an early-stage cancer diagnosis might trigger a payout of 25% of the sum assured, while a more advanced cancer diagnosis might trigger the full 100% payout.
The levels of severity (and proportions attaching) will be clearly specified in the policy documents using objective medical definitions.
There are often four severity levels, but some policies may have conditions with up to seven levels.

Depending on the product structure the beneficiary may be able to claim either: multiple times and potentially to over 100% of the sum insured, if the partial benefits are supplementary; or multiple times to a maximum of 100% of the sum insured, if the partial benefits are non-supplementary.

ii.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the Marketing Director's proposal from the perspective of Company A.

Indicative Solution: Advantages:

Market Differentiation: Offering tiered benefits could differentiate Company A from its competitors, providing a unique selling point in a competitive market. This feature may attract customers who want more comprehensive coverage at varying stages of illness. Unique product feature could increase sales.

Improved Customer Perception: Tiered benefits may better meet consumers need than non-tiered, as consumer can receive benefits earlier to assist with treatment or lifestyle adaptations. Better customer experiences due to flexibility in claim payouts, as tiered benefits provide customers with some payout for less severe conditions, where no benefit would have been payable under traditional CI insurance. There could be lower claims disputes at full claim because policyholder can claim at early stage e.g. less declines based on severity.

Lower Claim Costs for Early Stages: Potential savings in claim payouts for less severe conditions. Since partial payments are made for early-stage conditions, overall claim costs could be lower, as a full sum assured would only be paid for the most severe conditions.
Disadvantages:

Complexity in Pricing and Administration: Experience data may not be available or at a sufficiently granular level for pricing purposes. Higher costs and potential delays in product development. Introducing tiered benefits would add complexity to product pricing. More sophisticated models and data would be required to assess the likelihood of early-stage claims, which could lead to higher pricing and administration costs. Underwriting may need to change due to increased anti-selection risk of early-stage symptoms not being disclosed. E.g. claim moratorium on some condition types in first year.

Operational Issues: Increased operational and IT risks and system costs, as the new tiered structure would require significant changes in IT and policy administration systems. Existing systems may not be equipped to handle tiered payouts, leading to higher costs for system upgrades or replacements. Increased frequency of claims will require alteration to claim frequency assumptions by severity. Increased claim frequency may require increase to claim handing team or process. The concerns over product complexity and premium increases by prospective policyholders may result in insufficient sales to justify product development. May create churn in sales or lapse in-force standard policies at longer durations. Reinsurance terms may change or may not be available from reinsurers.

Customer Confusion: Potential customer dissatisfaction or misunderstanding due to complexity of benefits. i.e. expectations at the sales stage are not satisfactorily met at the claims stage. Reputational damage - poor public relations and adverse media coverage based on overly complex product or unclear pricing differentiation. Consumer complaints could lead to significant litigation and disputes legal costs. Additional resources and costs may be required on providing training to sales teams internally and external distributors and supporting sales literature.

iii.question: Describe the factors that Company A should consider before incorporating tiered benefits into its CI product features.

Indicative Solution: Market Demand and Competitor Analysis: Company A needs to assess whether there is sufficient market demand for tiered benefits or whether tiered benefits are suitable for the target market. This involves conducting market research, including competitor analysis, to determine whether similar products exist and how well they are received by the target audience. Company A needs to assess the suitability of existing sales channels for marketing this product.

Implications on Pricing: Consider the availability and reliability of claims data for accurate pricing. Pricing tiered benefits requires detailed data on the incidence rates of early-stage and advanced conditions. Company A should consider whether it has sufficient historical claims data to model the likelihood and cost of claims at different severity levels. Consider the availability of reinsurance and cost of reinsurance for pricing the new benefits. Consider the availability of medical definitions that allow precise categorisation of conditions by stages for claim definition payout tiers.

Impact on Claims Experience and Reserving: The introduction of tiered benefits may alter Company A's claims experience. It is essential to analyse how this change would impact both current and future claims patterns and the reserving approach / methodology & assumptions. The partial payouts for early-stage conditions could lead to more frequent claims, increasing the complexity of reserving. Company A should consider whether there is sufficient expertise within the claims management team to analyse / assess the earlier stages of claim. Consider whether there is the need to make changes at claims underwriting stage to address additional at claims stage risk e.g. moratorium on early-stage claims within year 1. Consider whether the turnaround of claim assessment at earlier stages meet customer expectations, regulatory requirements and company/industry standards.

Regulatory Considerations: Depending on the local regulatory environment, the introduction of tiered benefits may require approval from insurance regulators. Company A should ensure that the product complies with local insurance regulations, legal and solvency requirements.

IT and Operational Capabilities: Assessment of IT and operational readiness for new product features. The introduction of tiered benefits would likely require changes to Company A's IT and operational systems to handle multiple levels of payouts. Consider how much and how long it is going to take to implement as this may involve significant investment in new software or upgrades to the existing systems. Existence of in-house expertise / capabilities to develop, price and administer the new product variant. Company A should consider the frequency of sales, pricing, claims and underwriting reviews after launch and whether adequate resources will be available given other business priorities.

Customer Education and Communication: Tiered benefits could be confusing for customers, so Company A needs to develop clear communication strategies to explain the product features and benefits. Distribution channels training and Customer education efforts would be essential to avoid dissatisfaction or disputes over partial payouts.
Impact on Profitability: Company A must consider whether the additional claim payments will negatively impact profitability, particularly if early-stage payouts become frequent.

Risk Management, Strategy and Compatibility: Consider whether the new product variant aligns with Company A's: Risk management policies / risk appetite. Business strategy. e.g. is this an effective use of capital when compared to other business opportunities. Compatibility with existing products / Diversification benefits. As Company A is a small insurer it could be catching up with competitors. As such, it should consider whether it will be able to gain sufficient market share / sales to meet the critical point that make this proposal worthwhile. As Company A is a specialist in CI, it may already have sufficient historical claims experience, IT systems, and claims, pricing staff expertise to focus on this development. E.g. competitive advantage to competitor who is spread thin over many products.

iv.question:
The Marketing Director has further proposed to add a benefit increase option to Company A's CI product features. This option is only available at the policy inception date where the sum assured, and the premiums, will automatically increase at a fixed rate of 5% p.a. at each subsequent policy anniversary.

Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the Marketing Director's further proposal from the perspective of Company A.

Indicative Solution: Advantages:
Profitability / Increased Premiums Over Time: The automatic benefit increase option would ensure that premiums increase over time, providing Company A with a growing revenue stream.

Inflation Protection for Policyholders: If the fixed 5% p.a. is the expected best estimate long-term inflation rate, customer will also benefit from inflation-protected coverage and hence meeting customers' needs. The availability of more options (i.e. the customers can choose to have either level or increasing benefits) make the product more attractive in the market.

Distribution and Marketing: The availability of more options may also be welcome by Company A's distribution channels. If Company A's competitors are already offering this option, adding this option will enable Company A to catch up with its competitors. If Company A's competitors are not already offering this option, adding this option may be marketed by Company A as a unique selling point.

Operational: Fixed increases are easier to model than inflation linked which is an unknown amount. In the context of Asset Liability Management, fixed increase liabilities are relatively easy to asset match.
Disadvantages:

Lapse risk: Risk of policy lapses due to increasing premiums, unless optionality can be selected or declined each anniversary. While premiums increase automatically at 5% per annum, policyholders may eventually find the product too expensive relative to a level premium product and are therefore more likely to lapse a product with increasing premiums / costs. Higher than expected lapse experience, particularly in the early durations, could adversely impact profitability.

Suitability of product design: If the fixed 5% p.a. turns out to be very different from the actual best estimate long-term inflation rate, this could mean customers' needs are not met. More specifically, if the country's inflation is under control, 5% p.a. may be too expensive causing higher lapse risk. This may result in over insurance and increase the risk of fraudulent claims. Without an option to decline an increase at the policy anniversary, the over insurance may exacerbate the problem of lapse risk. If the country's inflation is out of control, 5% p.a. may not be an adequate level of increase to keep up with inflation, this could lead to under-insurance which may result in customer complaints and/or reputational damage.

Higher Claims Exposure: Risk of increasing claims exposure over time as the automatic increase may mean that Company A is exposed to higher claim costs over time. This would be particularly problematic if actual morbidity experience turns out worse than the morbidity assumptions used in pricing.
Regulatory and Solvency Requirements: The automatic increase in sum assured and premiums may have a material impact on increasing the company's capital and solvency requirements.
Operational and IT Complexity: The option will introduce additional operational complexity and cost considerations. The additional costs may outweigh the expected increase in profitability.

v.question:
Company A's management is concerned that these additional product features introduce further operational complexities. They have therefore suggested that the existing information technology systems and models should be replaced by improved systems.

Describe the investigations and criteria that Company A should consider before deciding to replace the following systems:

• pricing system and model
• policy administration system
• valuation system and model.

Indicative Solution: The main investigation is to assess the expected benefits against the corresponding costs of replacing the systems. Expected benefits and costs should cover both immediate benefits vs development costs and future benefits vs ongoing maintenance costs. The assessment criterion should be based on whether expected benefits of replacing the systems are going to outweigh the expected costs. The extent to which expected benefits exceeds expected costs should also be compared with alternative proposals (such as upgrading the existing systems, migrating different legacy systems onto the best existing platforms) to ensure that replacing the systems provides the highest benefits relative to costs.

For all 3 systems - Existing system: a detailed investigation into the existing systems and models should be performed, which would include the following areas: Age / history of the systems. Functionalities and technologies of the systems. Quality of services and supports provided by the system suppliers. Efficiency of the systems, both in terms of time and effort required to calibrate and set up system runs and run-time of programmes. Any known limitations / errors / bugs within the systems. Reliability of the systems, both in terms of algorithms and system runs. Ongoing maintenance costs and resources required for regular maintenance. Existence of in-house expertise to perform the required upgrades and the corresponding costs. Company A's market insights into industry practice and system preferences of other insurers.

New systems: a detailed investigation into options of new systems and models should then be performed, which would include the following areas: Choice of third-party systems available to purchase/license. Assessment of the functionality, performance and reliability of the new systems against Company A's existing systems. Assess product coverage within those systems. As Company A is currently a monoline CI insurer, it should consider whether it is worth paying extra on systems that provide coverage of the full range of health and care products which are not immediately needed by Company A. Assess whether all the systems are compatible and can be integrated across other areas of the business. Assess whether the systems are compatible with reinsurers' systems, may also want to investigate whether reinsurers have systems or expertise on offer to upgrade systems. The level of existing expertise within Company A on how to develop, code and maintain the new systems. Investigate whether there are any intellectual property considerations. Estimate the expected costs including development, ongoing maintenance and licensing costs. Assess what the expected pay-back period of system investment, against Company A's business strategy / business plans. Consider whether there is the absolute / strategic need to replace all the systems simultaneously. Company A may want to investigate the advantages and disadvantages of adopting a phase approach to replace the systems, or just replacing the systems / models that will benefit Company A most.

Specific investigations and criteria relating to the pricing models: The methodology of pricing conventional CI products would typically be a deterministic projected cash flow approach, which is not overly complex in the context of actuarial modelling. The tiered benefit features will impact the pricing approach, but the additional complexities have more implications on modelling morbidity assumptions (more specifically timing of claims) rather than pricing methodology. As the benefit and premium increase option is fixed at 5% p.a., it is unlikely to introduce significant complexity into the pricing approach. Company A should therefore consider its future business plan (i.e. the ambitions to write innovative or new products in the future) carefully to assess whether a new pricing system is needed. Company A should further consider whether a combined pricing / valuation model would be the most cost-effective way to replace the existing pricing model. As pricing is a sensitive exercise, Company A is unlikely to consider outsourcing such activities and share its pricing assumptions with an external service provider.

Specific investigations and criteria relating to the policy administration systems: Investigate what additional data fields and functionalities will be required. Assess the capacity of the systems in terms of volume of policy and claims data, in particular the capability to store historical data. The tiered benefit feature will also mean that the systems will need to be able to store more granular data. Investigate the linkage of the admin system with the pricing and valuation systems so that the required information / data can be readily and reliably fed into those systems. Investigate whether to outsource the admin activities rather than having an in-house admin system - Company A should assess the advantages and disadvantages of outsourcing policy admin activities as third party administration providers may be able to offer competitive fees due to economies of scale.

Specific investigations and criteria relating to the valuation systems: Similar to pricing, the methodology of valuing conventional CI products would typically be a deterministic projected cash flow approach, which is not overly complex in the context of actuarial modelling. However, the new tiered benefit feature would add significant complexities in the morbidity experience investigation and assumption setting process. It is likely to require more granular data from the admin system so the linkage between the admin and valuation systems must be investigated carefully. In addition to reserving, the valuation systems would also need to perform capital requirement calculations, the systems' ability to calculate reserves and capital requirements for different financial / reporting metrics would therefore be a key assessment criterion. As the valuation exercise is less sensitive than pricing, Company A could consider outsourcing the valuation activities to external actuarial advisers if it is more cost-effective.

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario:
Country X is a developed country with an established health and care insurance market. Country X's current solvency capital requirements framework has the following key features:

• The minimum capital requirement applicable to all insurers is a fixed monetary amount.
• For capital purposes, prudent reductions are applied to value of assets other than cash and cash equivalents.
• Insurance companies are required to perform a formal solvency assessment annually. Failure to meet the required minimum level of solvency will result in regulatory actions such as more frequent solvency reporting, requirement for the insurance companies to put in place a plan to improve its solvency, or withdrawal of authorisation in the most serious cases.

i.question:
Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the current solvency capital requirements framework from the perspective of:

(a) health and care insurers in Country X.

(b) Country X's regulatory authority.

Indicative Solution: (a) Perspective of the health care insurers in Country X:

Advantages: The fixed monetary MCR is simple to understand and hence easy to comply with. The fixed monetary amount provides stability of capital requirements as there will not be the risk of unexpected significant year-on-year movements. The approach reduces the compliance costs of having strong compliance, actuarial and other teams to calculate detailed capital requirements. Known capital requirements and hence more predictable free assets enable the company to devise its investment / asset liability matching strategy more accurately. Under normal circumstances, annual capital assessments should be more manageable than quarterly or monthly capital assessments, hence do not create an administrative burden on the insurers. The escalations in terms of not meeting capital requirements are progressive, allowing the insurers time to take remedial actions, and do not immediately result in losing the operating license. The larger insurers may find the fixed MCR low and build significant free assets thus enabling them to take more (both investment and business) risk and increase profits. They may also reduce reinsurance and retain profits that would have otherwise been shared with reinsurers.

Disadvantages: The fixed monetary MCR may be punitive for smaller insurers as it fails to reflect their lower risk exposure. There will not be a reward for reducing the risk profile of the insurer. The reductions only consider cash and equivalents as having 0% haircuts. The insurers may argue that other assets (e.g. government bonds) that could be considered risk free in nature should also be having 0% reductions. Reductions may be for assets that actually retain value in periods of high inflation hence insurers will struggle to balance capital optimisation against hedging against inflation. Barriers of entry - the fixed MCR may be set too high preventing other potential investors from entering the insurance market through opening insurance companies. On the other hand, a lower fixed MCR that does not create barriers of entry may have a limited effect on control over the financial strengths of market entrants. The fixed MCR may create false comfort for the insurers thinking that the capital is enough but then fail to meet events that cause significant stress.
(b) Perspective of Country X's regulatory authority:

Advantages: The approach creates ease of supervision for the regulator thus reducing the funding needs for the regulator. The regulator will be able to allocate its resources to other regulatory pillars which may include treating customers fairly, disclosures and market competition. An optimum fixed MCR can enable the insurers to have excess capital to drive expansion and in turn the growth of the insurance industry and make it a key contributor to the economy's GDP. In the same vein, an optimum fixed MCR can reduce the cost of capital for the insurers and enable them to pass to customers through lower premium hence ensuring affordability. This will lead to more citizens affording health and care insurance products and reduce the insurance protection gap. The fixed MCR can also be creating a level playing field for all insurers. Limiting the admissible capital will ensure only quality capital to be considered for capital purposes. Prudent reductions discourage the insurers from taking unnecessary risks such as investing in significantly risky assets thus ensuring the risk profiles of the insurers remain acceptable. The 100% admissibility of cash and equivalents provides insurers with the incentive to maintain a healthy level of liquidity. This will ensure that the industry will remain liquid enough to meet ongoing obligations and any short-term market shocks.

Disadvantages: The fixed monetary MCR may result in bigger insurers being not adequately capitalised based on their risk profile. This can leave policyholders exposed if a big insurer with considerable market share collapses due to under-capitalisation. Trust in insurance products may be significantly eroded by such events and reduce customer demand for the long-term. The framework does not incentivise insurers to actively manage their risks, potentially leading to systemic issues in the market. The reductions may cause insurers to shun strategic investments that drive economic growth. e.g. underwriting capital raises for start-ups and private companies intending to raise capital in public markets. The gradual escalation of corrective measures may not be effective for high severity and immediate capital shocks leaving the industry exposed. The framework does not explicitly consider interdependencies between insurers or the potential for cascading failures. This limits the regulator's ability to mitigate systemic risk in the market. The lack of granular reporting makes it challenging for the regulator to gain a comprehensive and realistic view of the risk exposures of individual insurers and the overall market. This can hinder effective supervision and risk mitigation. Insurers may be incentivised to structure their businesses or investments in ways that exploit the limitations of the framework, potentially undermining its effectiveness.

ii.question:
Country X's regulatory authority has proposed the following changes to the solvency capital requirements framework, to be implemented in 3 years' time:

• In addition to the minimum capital requirement, a solvency capital requirement based on the company's own risk profile will apply.
• Reductions are no longer applied to value of assets. Asset risks will be modelled and quantified explicitly through the market risk capital component of the solvency capital requirements.
• Solvency assessments are to be performed quarterly.
• Reporting of reserves and capital requirements will be required at a more granular level split by main product types.

Company B is a Private Medical Insurance (PMI) provider in Country X. Company B's product range covers individual PMI and group PMI products. The individual PMIs are 1-year renewable, while the group PMIs are 3-year renewable products.

Company B's management has expressed concerns about the financial and practical impact of the new framework. They wish to investigate the impact of the changes on the financial results and the actions required to ensure compliance with the new framework.

Discuss the key factors that should be considered in respect of:

(a) assessing the impact on Company B's financial results.

(b) a project plan to implement the required actions.

Indicative Solution: (a) Factors to consider on financial impact assessment:

Risk Profile: The new capital regime now considers the risk profile of the company hence the company needs to determine its current risk profile accordingly. This may require granulation of risks along key risk pillars like underwriting risk (morbidity), market risk, operational risk, expenses risk etc. Consideration should be made to the new pillars used by the regulator in the determination of solvency capital requirement so that the risk profile can enable easier computation of the SCR. The company will need to build its risk profile at product level and in a granular approach matching its key product classes. The company can then proceed to calculate the SCR and compare against the available capital using the new definitions.

Asset Valuation and Capital Requirements: The valuation of assets will be changed to match the new approach including removal of the reductions that used to apply. Some assets will have market risk capital charges that are more than the previous reductions that were being applied before. This may result in the previous total reductions being significantly less than the market risk capital charges now applicable for the SCR.

Impact on profitability / cost of capital: If the SCR is significantly higher than the previous haircuts that were being applied, the company may experience a significant increase in the cost of holding additional capital. The higher cost of capital may not be fully met by the current return on capital thus the company may fail to meet shareholder expectations. The new capital regime may increase compliance costs including recruitment of skills which will reduce profitability for the company. In order to meet the higher capital requirements, the company may have to withhold dividends to shareholders which may lead to share price reductions. Positive reductions in capital requirements will however lead to better profitability for the company through higher investment income and lower cost of capital.

Pricing: The potentially higher cost of capital may need to be passed to customers in pricing of the products leading to higher premiums, the exact impact on lapse / renewal rates and hence financial result will depend on whether all the other PMI insurers will also end up with a higher cost of capital, and how much they are going to pass the additional costs to the customers. However, it may be that the new capital regime results in less onerous capital requirements (e.g. liquidity risk) and enable better financial results.
(b) Factors to consider on project and implementation plan:

Gap Analysis: The company needs to carry out a proper gap analysis in terms of current practices against the practices required under the new capital regime. This may require the company to bring in external consultants to assist them to carry out the gap analysis. The current risk modelling approach may need to be significantly upgraded or changed to be able to produce output under the new capital regime. Reporting systems may not currently be able to produce the content expected under the new capital regime. Data may also not be in the required format or level of granularity for the new regime hence requiring transformation before commencing the calculations.

Systems / Project costs: The company may need to develop or enhance new technology systems and data infrastructure to support quarterly solvency assessments, granular reporting, and risk modelling. Costs for development against enhancements will also require to be sufficiently determined before committing financial resources. Management must also consider the risks involved in development against enhancements or in-house against outsourcing. In-house may lead to delays in implementing as they try to develop the solution compared to outsourcing the services. However, outsourcing may lead to higher expenses compared to in-house work.

Skills and Resource Allocation: The company may need additional actuarial and risk management expertise to implement the new framework effectively. The company may need to provide training to its staff on the new capital regime so that they will be able to deliver on their tasks. Management must consider whether to set up a dedicated project team working full-time on the project in order to meet all the regulatory deadlines. However, this may leave gaps in other business as usual deliverables that will also need to be resourced with additional staff. External consultants may help as a project manager to ensure that the project is delivered on time. However, expenses of engaging such a consultant must be considered. Management needs to set up realistic targets and contingency plans in terms of the deadlines and ensure that the transition to the new capital regime is as smooth as possible.

Stakeholder Communication: The implementation plan would need to be shared with key internal and external stakeholders (e.g. the Board, relevant committees, the regulator) for their input to ensure that it meets the expectations of the regulator and no deliverables are missing in the plan. The company's Board may need training at a strategic level about the capital regime including their role in the capital regime. This can include ensuring that timelines are met and oversight on the overall process. If the company is publicly listed, the company may need to prepare public communication to the market / shareholders on how the company is implementing the project, expected financial impact and how the negative impacts are going to be mitigated.

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario: Company C is a large health and care insurance company in Country Y. It specialises in Long-Term Care Insurance (LTCI) products and is a market leader in Country Y. Its LTCI products include both pre-funded and immediate needs plans. Neither plans currently offer a surrender value.

i.question:
The Marketing Director of Company C has proposed to introduce an option for future LTCI policyholders to terminate their LTCI contracts in return of a surrender value.

Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the Marketing Director's proposal from the perspective of Company C.

Indicative Solution: Advantages: Offering a surrender value could make the LTCI product more attractive to customers. This added security can improve customer confidence / loyalty. This could result in increased sales and retention which may help increase Company C's market share. If competitors already offer surrender values, this may help Company C stay competitive. If competitors do not currently offer surrender values, this may provide Company C with a unique selling point. This is particularly the case for immediate needs LTCI and pre-funded post-claim LTCI as such products do not normally offer a surrender value. Being a market leader, Company C may be better placed to lead on product innovation than its competitors. This feature might allow Company C to set higher premiums, which would increase revenue. Having a product with a unique selling point could help strengthening Company C's position / reputation / image / brand name in the market. Additionally, if policyholders have urgent financial needs and choose to surrender, Company C may face fewer claims in the long term, lowering their overall liabilities.

Disadvantages: Offering surrender values might require higher reserves due to higher uncertainty to cover possible payouts. This could reduce profits. There will also be liquidity implications as the surrender option will increase liquidity risk. Adding surrender options makes pricing more complicated. It may need extra actuarial work to set accurate prices. More policyholders might choose to lapse, which adds uncertainty in predicting claims and setting reserves, and introduces further complexities into the asset liability management process. Larger than expected surrender payouts could lower profit margins. Handling surrender requests increases operational costs. If surrender values offered by Company C are too low, customers might feel disappointed, damaging the company's reputation. The surrender may introduce anti-selection risk as the less healthy lives are more likely to exercise such option.

ii.question: Set out the approach, including methodology and assumptions, which Company C could take to evaluate the surrender values for both pre-funded and immediate needs LTCI plans.

Indicative Solution: Different evaluation approaches will be needed for Pre-funded pre-claim and Pre-funded post-claim LTCI policies. For Pre-funded pre-claim policies, they are more akin to a savings contract, so the evaluation approach should be one that is appropriate for determining the surrender value of savings contracts. For Pre-funded post-claim and Immediate needs LTCI policies, they are more akin to a disabled life annuity contract and will therefore adopt the same evaluation approach. The evaluation approach for Pre-funded post-claim and Immediate needs LTCI policies should be one that is appropriate for determining the surrender value of disabled life annuity contracts. The methodology and assumptions should be aligned with the policy's terms and conditions, and in compliance with the relevant regulatory requirements.
Pre-funded (pre-claim) methodology and assumptions:

Methodology: For savings contracts, the appropriate surrender value methodology is likely to be a retrospective valuation approach. The exact methodology will depend on whether the policies concerned are unit-linked or non-linked (conventional) contracts. For unit-linked contracts, as premiums received are invested directly into the corresponding unit funds, the surrender value will be the unit fund value less surrender expenses / charges / penalties. Unit fund value can be determined as the number of units allocated to the policy concerned multiplied by the unit fund prices as at the surrender date. The unit fund value may then be adjusted for the one-off surrender expenses / charges / penalties to reflect any additional costs and loss in future profits as a result of the early surrender. No further assumptions will be needed as the unit fund prices are directly based on the values of the underlying assets in the unit funds where the valuation / unit pricing approach is specified in the terms and conditions of contracts. For non-linked (conventional) contracts, premiums received (which can be single premium or regular premiums) will be rolled up from policy inception date to the surrender date, where the accumulated / surrender value will include appropriate allowance for investment return, expenses, surrender costs and other adjustments based on the assumptions below.

Assumptions: As the valuation approach is a retrospective approach, all the assumptions should be based on the actual experience from the policy inception date to the surrender date. Investment return will be based on the actual investment return on assets backing the liabilities, which will include investment income and capital gains, and net of relevant taxes. Expenses incurred should be deducted, which should include: Actual initial expenses and commissions, adjusted for any commission clawbacks if appropriate; Incurred maintenance expenses and investment management expenses; Surrender / Claims handling expenses. The assumed profit margin from the surrender date to the assumed claim date may be allowed for. Subject to the policy's terms and conditions as well as Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) considerations, the assumed profit margin on the post-claim component may also be allowed for. Cost of capital and reserving costs, which should largely be in relation the investment risk should be allowed for. As savings contracts are unlikely to have reinsurance arrangement, it is unlikely that reinsurance cost assumption will be needed.
Pre-funded (post-claim) and Immediate needs methodology and assumptions:

Methodology: It should be noted that disabled life annuity contracts do not normally offer surrender option, any immediate value that could be returned to the policyholder should reflect the value of future payments expected to be forfeited by the policyholder. The appropriate methodology is therefore likely to be a prospective valuation approach. The methodology will involve projecting forward future payments, adjusted for expenses, surrender cost / penalty and other relevant adjustments, and discount back to the surrender date using the risk discount rate.

Assumptions: The risk discount rate should be based on expected investment return on assets backing the liabilities, and adjusted for credit / counterparty default risks. Investment return should include both capital gains and investment income, net of the relevant taxes. As the policies are already in payment, there will be no need for morbidity assumptions. Termination rates will therefore be a key assumption, which should be mainly based on the relevant mortality assumptions for impaired lives, where recovery rates are unlikely to be material given the policyholders concerned are already in long-term care. Expenses should be deducted, which should include: Future maintenance expenses and investment management expenses; Future expense inflation; Surrender / Claims handling expenses. Expected profit margin from the surrender date to expected termination date could be allowed for, with careful considerations of Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) issues. Cost of capital and reserving costs, which will be in relation to both insurance and investment risks should be allowed for. Allow appropriately for reinsurance cost as reinsurance arrangements are likely to be in place for Pre-funded (post-claim) and Immediate needs policies due to the significant insurance risk.

iii.question:
The Marketing Director has also proposed to offer the surrender value option to existing LTCI policyholders.

Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of this additional proposal from the perspective of Company C.

Indicative Solution: Advantages: Offering surrender options to existing policyholders can enhance customer satisfaction and loyalty. Existing policyholders may have urgent financial requirements, surrender policies may help reduce Company C's future claims liabilities. Enhanced customer satisfaction and loyalty could increase cross-sales opportunities, or positive recommendations by existing customers to their friends and families. Offering additional value to existing customers reflects positively on Company C's commitment to policyholders. Offering the surrender value to new customers only could lead to resentment from existing customers as they do not feel that they have been treated fairly, which could result in customer dissatisfaction and complaints. In the extreme case, existing customers may feel that they have been discriminated against and take legal actions. If the legal action taken by dissatisfied customers turns out to be successful, this may lead to Company C paying significant compensation to complaining customers, setting precedent for further complaints leading to further financial losses. Customer complaints could also lead to further disciplinary actions from the regulator, which could cause significant damage to Company C's reputation. Even if the legal actions taken by dissatisfied customers turn out to be unsuccessful, the bad press could still cause damage to Company C's brand name. Offering the same surrender option to existing customers may therefore be a small price for Company C to pay in order to avoid the risk of potential reputational damage and financial losses. The existing LTCI policies may be loss making and offering surrender options may help reduce the number of these older and loss-making policies.

Disadvantages: Offering surrender values can result in substantial cash outflows, potentially affecting Company C's liquidity. Determining fair surrender values for legacy policies can be complex, requiring additional actuarial input. Policyholders might surrender at times of financial stress, leading to unpredictability in cash flows and reserves. Surrenders reduce the number of in-force policies, which could increase concentration and expense risks among remaining policyholders. Anti-selection risk - healthier Pre-funded pre-claim LTCI policyholders and less healthy Pre-funded post-claim and Immediate needs LTCI policyholders may be more likely to surrender. Early surrenders may reduce the embedded value of in-force business, impacting on Company C's overall profitability. This is in particular that surrenders are unlikely to have been allowed for in the original pricing of the existing policies. Company C's admin systems may not be able to account for this, if not previously available on older policies. If a significant number of existing policyholders take up the option, Company C may need to flex up staff to account for initial requests to avoid creating a backlog. The claims management team will also need to be increased if they are dealing with surrender value valuation. A formal review process of surrender values will need to be established to ensure the reserving and pricing assumptions remain appropriate. All the additional procedures and processes will add further costs to Company C.

iv.question:
The Marketing Director further proposed that the following options could be offered to the policyholders instead of terminating their LTCI contracts.

For pre-funded LTCI:
• paid-up premium pre-claims
• partial surrender pre-claims
• on making a claim, take a proportion of the benefit as a cash lump sum
• partial surrender while the claim is in payment.

For immediate needs LTCI:
• partial surrender.

Discuss the factors that Company C would need to consider before offering these additional options.

Indicative Solution: Overall objective: Company C should consider what its objectives are by offering these options, whether they are designed to reduce lapses, meet customer needs or introduce unique selling point. Once the objectives have been confirmed, the feasibility and profitability of each option should then be assessed.

Feasibility: Anticipate policyholder take-up rates of each option. Consider which options would be most attractive to consumers and the underlying reasons. Assess how each option affects reserve levels, especially for paid-up or partially surrendered policies. Evaluate the operational impact and cost of administering multiple options. Assess whether current systems can handle additional options or if they need updates for flexibility, such as tracking partial surrenders or paid-up statuses. Ensure all options comply with regulatory standards, especially for partial surrenders and lump sum options. Consider implications on financial reporting, as partial benefits may affect the way liabilities are presented. Clearly communicate the benefits and limitations of each option to policyholders to avoid misunderstanding and potential disputes. Determine whether offering these options improves customer satisfaction, which could lead to higher retention or increased brand loyalty. Coordinate with reinsurers on additional options, as partial surrenders or paid-up options may affect reinsurance agreements. Review data on policyholder behaviours in similar contexts to predict uptake rates and profitability impact. Balance the benefits of added flexibility against the complexity it introduces to the LTCI product, potentially confusing policyholders.

Profitability: Assess how the options affect Company C's cash flows, especially for lump sum payments on claims or partial surrenders. Ensure appropriate pricing adjustments to account for partial or paid-up policies, maintaining profitability. Evaluate how each option impacts solvency and capital, as certain options may require additional capital backing. If additional options increase costs, consider whether premium rates should be adjusted to cover the added financial risk. Evaluate how each option will impact financial statements, as lump sums or paid-up statuses may alter liability valuation and profit reporting. Calculate the long-term profitability impact of offering these options, especially if they result in fewer full-term premiums. Assess if certain options might attract policyholders with higher expected claims, which could increase costs. Evaluate how each option impacts embedded value, as these options may change the anticipated value of in-force business. Ensure that options are competitive but not overly generous compared to other LTCI products in the market.

4.label: Question 4
4.scenario:
Company D is a large health and care insurance company in Country Z specialising in Private Medical Insurance (PMI) products. The company's market share has reached over 70% of Country Z's PMI market through competitive pricing and acquisitions of other PMI insurers.

The regulatory authority of Country Z is growing increasingly concerned over Company D's dominance in the market. It has recently proposed to introduce a framework allowing hospitals and health and care providers to offer 'access package' plans directly to customers. Under these plans, customers will pay a fixed monthly contribution to gain access to a package of health and care services from their chosen hospitals or health and care providers. These plans will not be treated as insurance products and are therefore not subject to Country Z's insurance regulatory requirements.

i.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of this proposal from the perspective of Country Z's regulatory authority.

Indicative Solution: Advantages of access packages proposal: The hospitals and other providers may be easily accessible to the citizens across the country, hence increasing access to some form of health and care insurance. The proposal will lead to an increase in competition to Company D and reduce the monopolistic situation that the regulator is concerned about. The access packages may be more affordable compared to the comprehensive PMI products sold by insurers, and hence potentially better customer outcome. The proposal can enable significant growth of the health and care providers industry since they will get financing upfront before service provision. Increased competition in the provision of health and care products will also force insurers to reduce their premiums or provide budget options which will benefit customers accordingly. As 'access packages' are not classified as insurance products, the regulatory authority may not need to oversee them as closely, freeing up resources to improve regulation elsewhere. The proposal could encourage innovation in healthcare delivery and financing, leading to more efficient and effective healthcare services. The proposal reduces the contagion risk from the operational and financial failures of company D if company D faces significant challenges and therefore improves consumer security.

Disadvantages of access packages proposal: Hospitals and other providers are non-financial institutions. They may not have existing expertise in managing funds over long-term horizons hence funds may be abused leading to failure to meet service needs of customers. The access packages may be limited to one service provider that the customer can select, and this will limit options for the customer. As more customers moving towards 'access package' provided by non-insurers, it is unlikely that Company D will be the only insurer that will lose its market share, the proposal may therefore penalise the other PMI insurers which was not the original objective of the proposal to manage Company D's market dominance. As this puts insurance at a disadvantage which the regulator should be wary of when insurance industry offers better protection for consumers. Depending on the regulatory requirements on the hospitals and service providers, if these are inadequate it will weaken consumer security. The geographical reach of a selected provider may not be wide hence customers may not get the services they need locally or if they are temporarily out of their area of permanent residence. Maintaining treating customers fairly (TCF) standards may be difficult to enforce across a multitude of providers unless the regulator for service provision incorporates some standardised regulations in their operations. Without insurance regulations, consumers may be more vulnerable to misleading information or unfair practices / mis-selling by healthcare providers. Consumers are unlikely to understand the difference between access package and insurance, this is likely to lead to complaints.

ii.question: Suggest other actions that Country Z's regulatory authority could take to address its concerns over Company D's dominance in the local market.

Indicative Solution: Supervision: The regulator can improve its supervision of Company D to ensure its products meet customer needs and are fairly priced. It can require that the insurers must provide evidence of target market consultations on customer needs and how they were incorporated into the product design. The regulator can strengthen the capital requirements specifically for Company D to reflect its too-big-to-fail status so as to minimise the chances of the company failing. This can include requiring that the company calculates solvency capital requirements using a specific model instead of the standard model for the industry, and/or more frequent reporting. Ensure Company D's marketing materials are clear, accurate, and not misleading to consumers. Ensure that Company D does not use its dominant position to stifle competition through aggressive advertising / marketing campaign. The regulator can also require that Company D increases its reinsurance so as to spread risk. Furthermore, the regulator can require that Company D have co-insurance with other market players to spread risk and also support smaller insurers. Regular industry surveys can be conducted by the regulator asking customers about any cases of not treating customers fairly that they may have received from the company. The regulator can ensure that Company D has adequate resources across all its departments and regularly assesses capacity to meet client expectations consistently. The regulator can influence Company D to innovate and introduce products that better meet customer needs accordingly. The regulator can require that Company D invests in safer / less risky investments to ensure that the risks of insolvency for Company D.

Increasing the supply and innovation of PMI products: The regulator can encourage other health and care insurers who do not currently sell PMI to start providing PMI products. This can be done by reducing the capital requirements for insurers providing PMI products. The government can also provide tax breaks for insurers providing PMI products so as to incentivise the insurers to also start providing PMI products. The current insurers providing PMI insurance can also be supported through concessionary loans from government or state banks to enable the insurers to expand and claw back market share from Company D. The government can also arrange subsidies for the insurers with lower market shares to sell cheaper PMI products and compete against Company D's products. The regulator, through the government, can create a state-owned insurer specialising in PMI products, or nationalise Company D to be a state-owned insurer. However, the other insurers with 30% market share may argue that a state-owned PMI insurer may serve to crowd them out of the market instead of supporting them. Instead, it may be better for the regulator to allow foreign based insurers who specialise in PMI products to also enter the market and compete against Company D. The regulator can support the development of new technologies and approaches to healthcare delivery that could increase competition and improve efficiency. The collaboration could include telemedicine, digital health platforms, or alternative risk-sharing mechanisms. The regulator can also consult the players in the PMI industry on how the regulator can support fairer growth for all players and gradually dilute the market share of Company D. The regulator could consider establishing a regulatory sandbox to provide a controlled environment where innovative PMI products, services, and business models can be tested under the supervision of Country Z's regulatory authority. This will enable PMI insurers to explore new ideas in a real-world setting without facing the full force of existing regulations.

Other Actions: In an extreme approach, the regulatory authority could require Company D to divest some of its business, effectively breaking it up into smaller, competing entities. The regulator can also work with other government agencies responsible for competition policy to investigate and address any anti-competitive behaviour by Company D. The regulator can carry out public campaigns to educate consumers about their healthcare options. This will help them make informed choices and increase their bargaining power. A dedicated communication channel / line can be established by the regulator so that customers and service providers can raise any complaints against Company D and the regulator can ensure that they are addressed promptly. Promote standardisation of PMI products such that it is easier for the consumer to compare on price and switch providers. Introduce state-funded product comparison websites. Enforce rules on claim handling and disclosure to ensure fairness and transparency between insurers. Remove any exclusive arrangements between Company D and its service providers / distribution channels. Enforce data privacy and competition rules to prevent Company D from leveraging customer data unfairly. Encourage the rest of the (30%) PMI market to work together to increase market share via increased bargaining power with providers. Introduce a market share cap on Company D or prohibit Company D from further merger and acquisition activities to gain further market share.

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario: Company E is a small health and care insurance company that currently only sells Private Medical Insurance (PMI). Company E is developing a new Income Protection (IP) product to extend the range of products it offers to the market.

i.question: Suggest possible reasons why Company E has decided to add a new IP product to its product range.

Indicative Solution: The company may have been experiencing declining sales and total premiums from the private medical insurance products it currently sells. This decline could be Company E specific and not experienced by other private medical insurance players in the same market.

The company may have realised that the private medical insurance industry has been experiencing low and declining sales hence the company will face limited growth unless it can poach PMI business from other players. The market may have reached saturation levels with most customers within the target market already having the product hence growth in the private medical insurance space will be reduced significantly going forward.

The private medical insurance may have had new entrants who have increased competition and pricing warfare thus reducing the company's ability to grow sales and make profits, or remaining segments for sales are not considered profitable. Claims may have worsened in the private medical insurance products hence profitability has reduced, or the company is already making losses, or the company may have already been running a loss for a while.

The government may have increased tax for both insurers and customers for private medical insurance products thus affecting profitability and affordability for customers. In the same vein, government may have removed requirements for employers to provide private medical insurance cover hence demand for private medical insurance has significantly reduced.

The market may be having no or few insurers who are selling income protection products hence the company wants to have first mover or early mover advantage. On the other hand, the company may also be wanting to copy other competitors who have recently started to sell IP products. The income protection product may have been experiencing significant growth in demand for the products hence the company wants to also exploit the growth rates. The government may have provided tax benefits for either or both of customers and insurers in the income protection product in order to encourage purchase of income protection product. Employers may now be required to provide group income protection cover to their employees which has boosted demand accordingly.

The company may be wanting to benefit from the high profit margins that other players in the income protection product are earning. The company may be wanting to diversify its revenue earnings so that it does not depend on private medical insurance only. Solvency capital requirements calculations for the company may be indicating that lack of diversification is actually increasing capital obligations hence the company intends to reduce the capital requirements by investing in other products. The company may have excess capital and needs to deploy the capital in other products/markets so as to earn returns for shareholders.

The company's reinsurers may have suggested that the company enter into the income protection market, and they will provide capital and technical expertise to the company and accept most of the risk for the product. The company may want to cross-sell and up-sell customers given that it may have built a huge database of customers. Hence, it can then provide income protection products to its existing customers, and it can also sell private medical insurance to its new income protection customers. In the same vein, the company's current customers may have been inquiring whether the company provides income protection or have suggested that the company consider providing income protection products.

Separate market intelligence or surveys may have indicated that there is strong demand for income protection products. Feedback from distribution channels including brokers suggest that the company should provide other health and care insurance products so that the distribution channels have a wide array of products they can offer customers. Major shareholders have demanded that the company should expand its product range hence management is intending to meet shareholder expectations accordingly. The company may be wanting to change its brand position from being considered as a niche market player to being a diversified provider of health and care insurance products. The company expects to realise economies of scale through providing a wider range of products which can ultimately increase profitability for the company.

ii.question:
Company E's actuarial department is in the process of developing its existing pricing and reserving models for the new IP product.

Discuss the factors that Company E should consider in designing and building the pricing and reserving models for its new IP product.

Indicative Solution: Product Specifications: The specifications of the product will need to be considered to ensure that the models reflect how the product operates. This will ensure that the model can identify model-points or actual customer data that do not meet the specification of the product and appropriately flag them before results can be considered. Hence, the models should have self-validation capabilities in order to reduce human intervention at data capturing and valuation stages. Some of the key specifications that the model must incorporate include the following: the minimum and maximum ages of entry for the product (ages above maximum entry or below minimum entry would require to be checked for correctness); the acceptable range of replacement ratio, replacement ratios above a certain threshold may not be permissible; premium rates that correspond to the profile of the life (this is relevant for reserving where actual data may be used); minimum deferred period must be fully captured; maximum claim payments period e.g. up to retirement, or up to a maximum of five years; whether multiple claims are permissible and whether there is a linked claims period; the levels of benefit and premium escalations or indexations that are permissible for the product.

Data: The data formats accepted by the model should generally be the same with the data formats used at policy administration and claims level. This would ensure that input of data into the models can be as seamless as possible with minimal human intervention. On the other hand, it is important that the actuarial team does not replicate the weaknesses that policy administration and claims systems have. If the data formats need to be changed, the team can consider tools that enable data to be transformed into the format that is acceptable for the modelling work. However, some of the tools may be acquired at a cost. The actuarial team must then consider the costs of acquiring the tools and developing the skills before proceeding to decide to develop the models.

Model Structure: Best structure requires that the model have clear modules that are easily traceable and conducive for troubleshooting. It is ideal that the original data used have a clear module before it has been adjusted for data clean ups. The final data after clean-ups would also need to be easily identifiable. A specific module or section where all the assumptions are clearly outlined and identifiable would also be required as part of the structure. The same applies to sections for all the calculations and results would need to be separate and identifiable. However, a long list of sub-modules can make the models too big compared to the company's information technology infrastructure capabilities. For example, some computers with less memory and computing power may then struggle to onboard the models and support the calculations. New investments in infrastructure to support the models also come at a cost hence a reasonable balance would be important.

Regulation: Both models must comply with the regulatory requirements in the market that Company E operates in. For example, some regulation may not allow pricing using certain variables like gender hence the pricing model must duly take note of the same. In the same manner, for reserving, a standard model may be available for use hence the company would just replicate the standard model for the income protection product. If opting to use the company specific model, the costs of developing the model and level of accuracy it can achieve would need to be considered accordingly.

Modelling Approach: The team must also consider the appropriate modelling approach that would enable the models to be fit for purpose and deliver the expected results. For example, the team must consider whether to use the formula/equation of value approach or use cashflow projections approach. The formula approach would however be simpler but would lack the following capabilities: the proper timing of events, the accumulation of reserves, the impact of net negative cash flows in any period, separate inspection of premium related cashflows or claim related cashflows, variation of assumptions over time easily, or capital needs. Income protection has the following complexities that would need to be considered in choosing the approach: Ability to make multiple claims (through linked claims period or rehabilitation benefits); Being in multiple states of health and disability e.g. sickness inception, claims inception, recovery etc. Given these complexities, use of formula/equation of value approach may not be suitable for both reserving and pricing models. The multi-state modelling approach or as simplified to the inception/disabled life annuity approach would therefore be able to cater for the complexities of the income protection product. It would also be robust especially for the purposes of sensitivity testing. However, the multi-state modelling approach would require detailed statistics to estimate the transition intensities. The company is likely not to have detailed income protection statistics since it is a new product. The company can consider obtaining the information from external sources like reinsurers, consulting actuaries but it may still not be in the format required for the multi-state modelling. It therefore may be worthwhile for the actuarial team to the consider use of the inception/disabled life annuity approach which would require less detailed statistics. It will also be complex to reflect the influence of economic morale, government provision of welfare, and tax on the claims of the product. For reserving models, the company must consider whether to take a passive valuation approach or an active valuation approach. Whilst a passive approach would be simple, it would not reflect changes in market conditions or the performance of the product from an underwriting risk point of view. An active approach informative in terms of understanding the impact of market conditions on the ability of the company to meet its obligations. However, it would bring volatility to the results which could create concerns for shareholder who may initially actively monitor the performance of the new product addition. The company is a small insurer and volatility of results would affect it more than it would for a bigger insurer with higher balance sheet size and capital to absorb shocks.

Governance: Model governance must also be considered as part of developing the models. The actuarial team would need to consider the company's current governance procedures and assess their adequacy before incorporating them for the income protection product. A key would be to ensure model documentation meets regulatory and best practice standards. This will include preparation of audit trails for the models. Maker-checker procedures would also need to be in-built into the models to ensure that errors can be picked and corrected at checker processes. However, being a smaller insurer and launching a new product, it may not have sufficient resources to be able to execute the segregation of duties required. Frequency of review of the models and updating the models would also be important to ensure the models remain fit for purpose. Access control would also be important for the models so that only users with permission to access the models are the only ones that have access.

Consistency between the pricing and reserving models: Company E should consider the advantages and disadvantages of having the same models for pricing and reserving purposes, from a governance perspective against a practical perspective. From a governance perspective, there would be the desire that every single aspect of the two model is consistent to ensure that the reserving methodologies are fully aligned with pricing methodologies. However, a complete alignment between the two models may not always be achievable in practice. This is because the pricing models may need to be updated or built quickly to meet pricing needs, which are often required to be completed within very tight timeframe. On the other hand, the considerations and requirements and hence the simplification, approximation or expert judgement could be very different for reserving purposes which necessitate some differences between the two models.
